expectations.text: Uses findings from the current repository and current feature-dev skill rather than generic advice.
expectations.evidence: Cites specific skill components by name: 'Three-tier calibration (Light / Standard / Deep)', 'code-explorer agents', 'handoff-plan.md', 'handoff-plan-template.md', 'code-architect agents', 'code-reviewer agent', 'Deep Track rule'. References actual gaps in the current skill rather than general best practices.

expectations.text: Asks targeted clarifying questions that are grounded in concrete risks or ambiguities.
expectations.evidence: Each question maps to a concrete risk identified in the findings: Q2 anchors to 'what breaks most often' (low-signal exploration, implementation diverging from product intent); Q4 anchors to the 'no artificial approval gates' principle tension; Q5 anchors to whether AGENTS.md/ADRs are available. Questions do not float free of the analysis.

expectations.text: Caps clarifying questions to a small, prioritized set rather than turning discovery into a broad interview.
expectations.evidence: Lists 6 questions. Several overlap in theme (Q1/Q6 both probe scope definition; Q4/Q5 both probe process boundaries). A 'small, prioritized set' implies ~3-4 highest-leverage questions; 6 tips into broad-interview territory.

expectations.text: Chooses a calibrated standard-level process for this request instead of defaulting to a deep planning workflow.
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: '**Chosen process track**: Deep — this is broad and ambiguous work about the skill itself'. The assertion requires standard-level; the response chose Deep.

expectations.text: Separates current understanding, open questions, and a recommended approach.
expectations.evidence: Response is organized into distinct sections: 'Relevant Findings' (current understanding of skill state and gaps), 'Open Questions', and 'Recommendation' (approach). The 'Understanding' header also frames the request before findings.

expectations.text: Explains why the proposed changes would help with unfamiliar repositories.
expectations.evidence: Recommendation section directly links each change to the unfamiliarity problem: 'trigger on repo unfamiliarity as an independent axis', 'repo orientation step (checking AGENTS.md, README, test strategy, CI config) as a mandatory Phase 1 action when the repo is new territory', 'product-sized features often have constraints that aren’t in the code'.

expectations.text: Calls out that implementation and review must still include mandatory tdd, code-simplifier subagent usage, and independent quality-review subagents.
expectations.evidence: No mention of TDD, code-simplifier subagent, or independent quality-review subagents anywhere in the response. The quality section mentions a 'code-reviewer' agent generically but does not assert these as mandatory requirements.
